items.name: Max count of parts per partition across all tables

triggers.name: ClickHouse: Too many MergeTree parts
triggers.description:
Descease INSERT queries frequency.
Clickhouse MergeTree table engine split each INSERT query to partitions (PARTITION BY expression)
and add one or more PARTS per INSERT inside each partition,
after that background merge process run, and when you have too much unmerged parts inside partition,
SELECT queries performance can significate degrade, so clickhouse try delay insert, or abort it.

triggers.name: ClickHouse: There are queries running is long
ClickHouse: Replication lag is too high
triggers.description:
When replica have too much lag, it can be skipped from Distributed SELECT Queries without errors
and you will have wrong query results.

triggers.name: MSSQL: Percent of ad hoc queries running is high
triggers.description: The lower this value is, the better. High values often indicate excessive ad hoc querying and should be as low as possible. If excessive ad hoc querying is happening, try rewriting the queries as procedures or invoke the queries using `sp_executeSQL`. When rewriting isn't possible, consider using a plan guide or setting the database to parameterization forced mode.

triggers.name: MSSQL: Percent of times statement recompiles is high
triggers.description: This number should be at or near zero, since recompiles can cause deadlocks and exclusive compile locks. This counter's value should follow in proportion to "Batch Requests/sec" and "SQL Compilations/sec".

triggers.name: Oracle: Too high database concurrency
triggers.description: The concurrency rate exceeds `{$ORACLE.CONCURRENCY.MAX.WARN}`%. A high contention value does not indicate the root cause of the problem, but is a signal to review resource consumption (determine the "heaviest" queries in the database, trace sessions, etc.) This will help find the root cause and possible optimization points both in database configuration and the logic of building queries.

triggers.description: The number of locked sessions exceeding `{$ORACLE.SESSION.LOCK.MAX.TIME}` seconds is too high. Long-term locks can negatively affect the database performance. triggers.description:
Checkpoints are points in the sequence of transactions at which it is guaranteed that the heap and index data files have been updated with all information written before that checkpoint. At checkpoint time, all dirty data pages are flushed to disk and a special checkpoint record is written to the log file.

triggers.name: PostgreSQL: Required checkpoints occur too frequently
triggers.description:
Checkpoints are points in the sequence of transactions at which it is guaranteed that the heap and index data files have been updated with all information written before that checkpoint. At checkpoint time, all dirty data pages are flushed to disk and a special checkpoint record is written to the log file.
https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/wal-configuration.html

triggers.name: Redis: Memory fragmentation ratio is too high
triggers.description:
This ratio is an indication of memory mapping efficiency:
  - Value over 1.0 indicate that memory fragmentation is very likely. Consider restarting the Redis server so the operating system can recover fragmented memory, especially with a ratio over 1.5.
  - Value under 1.0 indicate that Redis likely has insufficient memory available. Consider optimizing memory usage or adding more RAM.

Note: If your peak memory usage is much higher than your current memory usage, the memory fragmentation ratio may be unreliable.

https://redis.io/topics/memory-optimization
